title: Śrī Ajitaśānti Stava (Second Smaraṇa)
summary: This is a 46-verse devotional hymn that praises two Tīrthaṅkaras together — Ajitanātha, the 2nd, and Śāntinātha, the 16th — weaving their names and virtues in alternating stanzas. Its very title is a play on meaning: 'Ajita' means 'the unconquered' and 'Śānti' means 'peace,' so the hymn asks for fearless, unconquered peace. Jains recite it as a protective prayer (smaraṇa) to calm fear, dissolve affliction, and ward off disease and obstacles. It is famous for its dazzling variety of poetic metres, a showcase of devotional craft.
ritualPlacement: The Ajitaśānti Stava is the **second of the Nine Smaraṇas (Nava Smaraṇa)** — the fixed set of protective stotras recited within the **Pākṣika (Pakkhī)** pratikramaṇa, after the two Nandī-sūtra sajjhāys and before the Pakkhī Khāmaṇā. Because the Cāumāsī (four-monthly) and Sāṁvatsarī (annual) pratikramaṇas carry **no independent text of their own** and are performed exactly as the Pakkhī — substituting the word 'pakkhī' with 'cāumāsī' or 'saṁvatsarī' and lengthening the kāyotsarga — this hymn is recited in all three of those observances. Verse 38 confirms this in the text itself: it is to be recited 'pakkhiya, cāumāse, saṁvacchariye' (at the fortnightly, four-monthly, and annual rites). It is not part of the daily Devasi or Rāi.
authorAndSource: Attributed in the book to **Nandiṣeṇasūri** (Nandiṣeṇa), a medieval Śvetāmbara monk-poet. The hymn praises Ajitanātha (2nd Tīrthaṅkara) and Śāntinātha (16th) in alternation. The bulk (vv.1–42) is in Prakrit/Apabhraṁśa across many metres; the closing four verses (vv.43–46) are well-known Sanskrit maṅgala stanzas appended in the recitation tradition (Sarva-maṅgala-māṅgalyaṁ; Upasargāḥ kṣayaṁ yānti; Śivamastu sarva-jagataḥ; Smaraṇaṁ yasya sattvānāṁ). The poet signs his own name in v.37 ('Nandiseṇa…abhinandi'). (See §3.5 of the tradition knowledge base.)
structureArc: vv.1–2 bow to both Jinas and declare the intent to praise (thosāmi). v.3 sounds the hymn's refrain — 'namo ajiya saṁtiṇaṁ,' homage to Ajita and Śānti — the bivalent name-play repeated throughout. vv.4–6 unfold their virtues and urge the listener to take refuge in them as bestowers of fearlessness (abhaya). vv.7–8 set the alternating pattern explicitly: v.7 hymns Ajita, v.8 hymns Śānti. v.9 opens the royal portrait — Śāntinātha's bodily and sovereign majesty in rich elephant-and-throne imagery. v.10 bows to Ajita; v.11 expands Śāntinātha's identity as the **cakravartin of Hastināpura** with the fourteen jewels, nine treasures, and vast retinue. v.12 prays to Śānti for peace. vv.13–18 compare the Jinas as surpassing moon, sun, Indra, and mountain-lord. vv.19–31 portray the cosmic assembly — gods, asuras, kinnaras, and especially the celestial women (devasundarī) — venerating, circumambulating, singing, and dancing at the Jinas' feet. vv.32–34 invoke the auspicious bodily marks (svastika, bull, lion, wheel) and the Jinas purified by tapas. vv.35–37 conclude the praise proper and pray for joy (nandi), where the poet names himself. vv.38–39 are the phala-stuti (statement of fruits): when and by whom it is recited, and the promise that diseases are destroyed. vv.40–42 give closing homage and the removal of sin. vv.43–46 are the appended Sanskrit maṅgala verses sealing the recitation.
recurringImagery: (1) **The double name as a single prayer.** The whole hymn turns on the pun in its title: Ajita ('unconquered, invincible') and Śānti ('peace'). Stanza after stanza yokes the two — 'ajiya-saṁti,' 'ajiyaṁ saṁtiṁ' — so that praising the two Tīrthaṅkaras simultaneously asks for one thing: an invincible, fearless peace. The refrain 'namo ajiya saṁtiṇaṁ' (v.3) anchors this motif. (2) **Alternation (Ajita / Śānti).** From v.7 onward the verses deliberately swing between the two Jinas — one stanza to Ajita, the next to Śānti — a structural call-and-response the reader should expect throughout. (3) **The four surpassings.** In vv.13–18 the Jina is repeatedly said to exceed the four crowns of worldly excellence: the autumn **moon** in gentleness (saumya/soma), the **sun** in radiance (teja), **Indra** (the lord of gods) in beauty (rūpa), and the **mountain-lord** in steadfast essence (sāra). (4) **The venerating celestial women (devasundarī).** vv.26–31 dwell at length on heavenly women — graceful as swans, jewel-adorned, dancing to flute and drum — who bow at the Jina's 'invincible feet' (suvikkamā kamā) again and again; this is a sustained tableau of devotion, not many separate scenes. (5) **Royal / cakravartin grandeur.** Śāntinātha was a universal emperor (cakravartin) before renunciation; vv.9 and 11 render his sovereignty through elephants, throne, the fourteen jewels and nine treasures — majesty surrendered for liberation.
keyThemes: Twin praise of Ajitanātha and Śāntinātha
Taking refuge (śaraṇa) for removal of suffering
Protective recitation against disease and obstacles
Cosmic assembly's devotion to the Jina
Surrender of royal cakravartin glory for liberation
Metrical virtuosity as an act of devotion

glossary.definition: A hymn of praise; a sustained eulogy of a Tīrthaṅkara.
A protective 'remembrance' stotra; here the second of the Nine Smaraṇas recited in the Pakkhī pratikramaṇa.
'The unconquered'; Ajitanātha, the 2nd Tīrthaṅkara — and, by word-play, the quality of invincibility itself.
'Peace'; Śāntinātha, the 16th Tīrthaṅkara — and, by word-play, the peace the devotee seeks.
A 'ford-maker'; an enlightened teacher who establishes the path across the ocean of rebirth.
A universal emperor possessing fourteen jewels and nine treasures; the worldly sovereignty Śāntinātha renounced.
Fearlessness; the gift of safety the Jinas bestow ('abhayakara').
Refuge; the act of taking shelter in the Jinas (v.6, 'saraṇaṁ pavajjaha').
Celestial women / goddesses who venerate the Jina's feet with song and dance (vv.26–31).
Affliction, calamity, or hostile obstacle; the hymn is invoked as 'upasagga-nivāraṇa,' a remover of such troubles (v.38).
Auspiciousness; the appended Sanskrit verses (vv.43–46) are classic maṅgala stanzas of benediction.
Joy, felicity; the prayer of vv.35–37, punning on the poet Nandiṣeṇa's own name.
The 'statement of fruits' — verses (38–39) naming when the hymn is recited and the benefits (freedom from disease) it confers.
The Pākṣika (fortnightly) pratikramaṇa, within which this smaraṇa is recited; also the rite from which Cāumāsī and Sāṁvatsarī derive by word-substitution.

howToRead: A new learner can simply hear the hymn as one prayer for 'unconquered peace,' letting the refrain 'namo ajiya saṁtiṇaṁ' carry the meaning. An advanced practitioner should watch the deliberate Ajita/Śānti alternation and savour the shifting metres — the hymn is celebrated as a metrical tour de force — while tracking the devotional arc from invocation, through the cosmic assembly's worship, to the phala-stuti and closing maṅgala.

sources.title: Stuti-Kāvya / Jain stotra anthologies (Ajitaśānti Stava recensions)
sources.consultedFor: Attribution to Nandiṣeṇasūri; identification of Ajitanātha (2nd) and Śāntinātha (16th); metrical-showcase character of the hymn.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)
sources.consultedFor: Ritual placement as Smaraṇa #2 within Pakkhī; pakkhī/cāumāsī/saṁvatsarī substitution; verbatim verse text.

notes: Verse text follows the book verbatim, including its orthographic inconsistencies (e.g. doubled/single conjuncts, variant anusvāra); these are preserved, not normalized. Verses 43–46 are Sanskrit maṅgala stanzas widely appended to the hymn in recitation; their individual authorship differs from Nandiṣeṇasūri and is not asserted here. Date for Nandiṣeṇasūri (commonly placed c. 12th–13th c. CE) is approximate and not independently fixed by the source book; treat as uncertain. Some manuscript traditions count this hymn at 47 verses; the book's numbering (46) is followed here. An asterisk marker appears in the book at vv.13 and 38 (footnote/variant cue) and is part of the printed text.
